summary:
The Mughal Decline (c. 1707–1858 AD) was the progressive collapse of the [[event:103|Mughal Empire]] from a centralized state governing most of the Indian subcontinent into an empty ceremonial shell whose last emperors were puppets of whoever controlled Delhi — a process accelerated by the disastrous reign of [[event:2507|Aurangzeb]], whose anti-Hindu policies alienated the empire's Hindu majority, whose Deccan wars bankrupted the state, and who died in 1707 having created the conditions for rapid imperial dissolution. The 50 years following Aurangzeb's death saw a dozen emperors assassinated, blinded, or deposed by competing factions of nobles and army commanders who competed for control of the imperial court while provincial governors became effectively independent rulers.

The [[event:2447|Nader Shah's invasion]] and sack of Delhi in 1739 was the most visible humiliation: the Persian conqueror's forces massacred tens of thousands of Delhi's inhabitants and carried off the Peacock Throne and Koh-i-Noor diamond along with immense treasure, demonstrating the empire's military impotence. The [[event:2368|Maratha Confederacy]] progressively took over administration of the northern plains; the [[event:2448|Third Battle of Panipat]] (1761) temporarily checked Maratha expansion but brought in the Afghan Durrani Empire as a new player. Regional powers — the Nawabs of Bengal, Hyderabad, Awadh, and Mysore — consolidated effective sovereignty in their domains. The [[event:2358|British East India Company]] exploited this fragmentation to expand from Bengal across the subcontinent; by 1803, the Mughal Emperor Shah Alam II had formally accepted British 'protection' — making the Mughals pensioners of the Company. The dynasty formally ended when Bahadur Shah Zafar, the last Mughal emperor, was deposed and exiled to Rangoon by the British after the 1857 Rebellion.
